1: skian gar echōn ho nomos tōn mellontōn agathōn, ouk autēn tēn eikona tōn pragmatōn — "for since the law has but a shadow of the good things to come instead of the true form of these realities" (skian echōn: having/holding a shadow — the shadow is the law's characteristic possession, not an accidental quality; ouk autēn tēn eikona: not the image itself — the law has a shadow but not the eikona [image, form, replica] of the things): the distinction between skia (shadow) and eikōn (image) is that the shadow lacks the substance and clarity of the image — both are less than the reality, but the image is a truer representation than the shadow. The Platonic background of copy-and-original is mediated here through Hellenistic Jewish thought.
4: adynaton gar haima taurōn kai tragōn aphairein hamartias — "for it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins" (adynaton: impossible — the same strong impossibility as Hebrews 6:4; the repeated adynaton is characteristic of Hebrews' categorical claims): the impossibility is ontological, not merely pragmatic — animal blood cannot remove moral guilt because the ontological gap between animal life and human guilt is unbridgeable. The repeated OT sacrifices implicitly acknowledged this — if they had achieved the removal, they would not need repeating (v2).
5: sōma de katērtisō moi — "but a body you have prepared for me" (katērtisō: you prepared, fashioned, made ready — perfect active, addressing God): the LXX of Psalms 40:6 reads sōma de katērtisō moi where the MT has ōzen koritha li (ears you have dug/opened for me — the idiom for God opening the ears for obedience). The LXX translator may have understood the opened ears as a metonymy for the receptive body (the obedient self), or may have been working from a variant Hebrew text. Hebrews uses the LXX reading to make the incarnation the fulfillment of Ps 40 — the Son comes in the prepared body to do God's will.
10: en hō thelēmati hēgiasmenoi esmen dia tēs prosphoras tou sōmatos Iēsou Christou ephapax — "and by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all" (hēgiasmenoi esmen: we have been sanctified — perfect passive periphrastic: the sanctification is a completed state with present reality; ephapax: once for all — the definitive, non-repeatable character of the offering): the thelemati (by the will) links back to Psalms 40:8 ("to do your will, O God") — the Son's will-doing is the mechanism of the community's sanctification. The body-offering that replaced the animal-sacrifices is the will-doing that the Psalm anticipated.
11: kai pas men hiereus hestēken kath' hēmeran leitourgōn kai tas autas pollakis prospherōn thysias, haitines oudepote dynantai perielein hamartias — "and every priest stands daily at his service, offering repeatedly the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins" (hestēken: stands — the standing priest is the image of unfinished work; contrast with the seated Son of v12): the Levitical priests' standing is not incidental but structural — there were no chairs in the sanctuary, because the work was never done. The contrast with the seated Christ (who sat down after offering one sacrifice for sins forever) makes the posture theologically significant.
19: echontes oun, adelphoi, parrēsian eis tēn eisodon tōn hagiōn en tō haimati Iēsou — "therefore, brothers and sisters, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus" (parrēsian: confidence, bold access, freedom of speech/entry — in the classical world: the citizen's right to speak in the assembly; in this context: the right to enter the sanctuary that was previously restricted): eisodon (entry, access) — the parrēsia is specifically an entry-right, not only an emotional confidence. The blood of Jesus is the basis of the access right, as the high priest's blood was the basis of his Day of Atonement access.
23: katechōmen tēn homologian tēs elpidos aklinē, pistos gar ho epangeilamenos — "let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering, for he who promised is faithful" (katechōmen: let us hold down, hold fast — same verb as Hebrews 3:6, 14: the community's grip on what was received; homologian: confession — same word as Hebrews 3:1; the community's public declaration of faith; aklinē: without bending, without inclining — rigid against pressure): the reason to hold fast is divine faithfulness, not human effort — the community holds its grip because the one who promised is trustworthy.
25: mē enkataleipontes tēn episynagōgēn heautōn, kathōs ethos tisin, alla parakalontes, kai tosoutō mallon hosō blepete engizousan tēn hēmeran — "not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near" (episynagōgēn: assembling together — a compound of syn- + agōgē: the bringing-together of the group; also used in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 for the eschatological gathering): the present tense participle indicates ongoing neglect by some within the community. Hēmeran (the Day): the eschatological Day of the Lord/Christ's return, whose proximity motivates intensified mutual encouragement.
26: hekousiōs gar hamartanontōn hēmōn meta to labein tēn epignōsin tēs alētheias, ouketi peri hamartias apoleipetai thysia — "for if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" (hekousiōs: willingly, voluntarily, deliberately — the contrast with the Levitical atonement of inadvertent sins [Hebrews 9:7, agnoēmatōn]; the deliberate, willful rejection of Christ's sacrifice leaves no atonement mechanism available): epignōsin tēs alētheias (the full knowledge of the truth) — this is not ordinary ignorance but knowledge-with-recognition of what has been received.
31: phoberon to empesein eis cheiras theou zōntos — "it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God" (phoberon: fearful, terrifying — predicate adjective; empesein eis cheiras: to fall into the hands of — the idiom of helpless subjection to a superior power): the formulation inverts the Psalter's comfort-language of "falling into God's hands" as divine mercy (2 Samuel 24:14 = falling into God's hands rather than human hands). Here the falling is judgment rather than mercy — the distinction being the attitude of the one who falls.
38: ho de dikaios mou ek pisteōs zēsetai, kai ean hyposteilētai, ouk eudokei hē psychē mou en autō — "but my righteous one shall live by faith, and if he shrinks back, my soul has no pleasure in him" (ek pisteōs zēsetai: will live from/out of faith — the same Habakkuk 2:4 citation as in Romans 1:17 and Galatians 3:11, but with different emphasis: Hebrews adds the negative counterpart "if he shrinks back"): hyposteilētai (shrinks back — aorist subjunctive of hypostellō: to draw back, shorten sails, retreat): the nautical imagery again — shrinking back is the failure to hold one's position, the pulling-back of the one who was moving forward.
2: epei ouk an epausanto prospheromenai, dia to mēdemian echein eti syneidēsin hamartiōn tous latreuontas hapax kekathrismenous — "for if they could have, would they not have stopped being offered? Because the worshippers, once truly cleansed, would no longer have been conscious of their sins" (syneidēsin hamartiōn: consciousness/conscience of sins — syneidēsis: the inner moral awareness, the shared-knowing of the self with itself; the consciousness of sin is the awareness that the guilt-problem remains unresolved): the argument is from silence converted to speech: the continued repetition of sacrifices is itself evidence of their incompleteness — if they had truly resolved sin, there would be nothing left to repeat. Hapax kekathrismenous (having been cleansed once): the hapax here is the goal never achieved under the old system but achieved by Christ.
3: all' en autais anamnēsis hamartiōn kat' eniauton — "instead, in those sacrifices there is an annual reminder of sins" (anamnēsis hamartiōn: a remembrance/memorial of sins — anamnēsis: active recalling, not passive memory; the word used in the eucharistic tradition [Luke 22:19; 1 Corinthians 11:24-25] for the remembrance-unto-presence that the Lord's Supper effects): the annual sacrifice functions as an anamnēsis of sins — a formal re-membering that the sin problem persists, an annual re-presentation of the guilt before God. Contrast with Hebrews 8:12: the new covenant promise is that sins will be remembered no more (ou mē mnēsthō eti).
6: holokautōmata kai peri hamartias ouk eudokēsas — "in burnt offerings and offerings for sin you took no pleasure" (Psalms 40:6 LXX, continued): ouk eudokēsas (you did not take pleasure — aorist: a completed act of divine non-pleasure): the divine dissatisfaction is not with the obedience that offered these sacrifices but with the inadequacy of animal sacrifice as a vehicle for the relational-moral restoration God desires. The Psalm reads God's heart as desiring obedience over sacrifice (cf. Micah 6:6-8; Hosea 6:6) — a prophetic tradition that Hebrews enlists to show the Levitical system was always interim.
7: tote eipon, Idou hēkō, en kephalidi bibliou gegraptai peri emou, tou poiēsai, ho theos, to thelēma sou — "then I said, See, I have come to do your will, O God, as it is written about me in the scroll of the book" (Psalms 40:7-8 LXX): en kephalidi bibliou (in the head/roll of the book — kephalis: scroll-roller, the end of the scroll where the title was; or the beginning/heading of the scroll): the Son identifies himself as the one about whom the entire scriptural scroll is written — not merely a verse here or there but the whole. Tou poiēsai... to thelēma sou (to do your will): the Son's incarnation is defined as will-doing — he comes not to perform animal sacrifices but to perform the will of God that the sacrifices symbolized.
8: anoteron legōn hoti Thysias kai prosphoras kai holokautōmata kai peri hamartias ouk ēthelēsas oude eudokēsas, haitines kata nomon prospherontai — "when he said above, You have not desired nor taken pleasure in sacrifices and offerings and burnt offerings and sin offerings — which are offered in accordance with the law" (kata nomon prospherontai: offered according to the law — the parenthetical clarification that these are the very offerings the Torah prescribes): the argument is careful: God commanded the Levitical system through Moses, yet through the Psalms God also says he does not desire those same offerings. The resolution is that the commandments governed the pedagogical interim; the Psalm reveals the heart that the system was always pointing toward.
9: tote eirēken, Idou hēkō tou poiēsai to thelēma sou; anairei to prōton hina to deuteron stēsē — "then he added, Behold, I have come to do your will. He removes the first covenant order in order to establish the second" (anairei to prōton: he removes/takes away the first — anairei: present tense, actively removing): anairō has the sense of lifting up and taking away — the same word used for executing/removing someone (Acts 2:23, Hebrews 5:33). The removal of the first is the act that creates space for the second. Hina to deuteron stēsē (so that he might establish the second — aorist subjunctive: a completed, decisive establishment): the two covenants cannot coexist permanently; the establishment of the second requires the removal of the first.
12: houtos de mian hyper hamartiōn prospheras thysian eis to diēnekes ekathisen en dexia tou theou — "but this priest, having offered one sacrifice for sins for all time, sat down at the right hand of God" (mian thysian: one sacrifice — the numerical contrast with the many; eis to diēnekes: in perpetuity, continuously, for all time — diēnekes: carried through without break, uninterrupted; a mathematical/administrative term for a continuous running total): ekathisen: sat down — the single aorist that the entire argument builds toward. The sitting is the posture of completed work; no chair in the sanctuary because no Levitical work was ever complete. The seated Son marks the ending of a priestly service that is not repeated.
13: to loipon ekdechomenos heōs tethōsin hoi echthroi autou hypopodion tōn podōn autou — "waiting from that time until his enemies are made a footstool for his feet" (ekdechomenos: waiting, expecting — present active participle: the Son's current activity is expectant waiting): the posture pairs sitting (completed sacrifice) with waiting (expected consummation). The session is not passive inactivity but active intercession (Hebrews 7:25) combined with sovereign expectancy. Hypopodion tōn podōn autou (footstool for his feet — Psalms 110:1): the language of absolute dominion; the footstool-enemy is the one fully subdued under the victor's feet in ancient Near Eastern victory imagery.
14: mia gar prosphora teteleiōken eis to diēnekes tous hagiazomenous — "for by a single offering he has brought to completion for all time those who are being made holy" (teteleiōken: he has brought to completion, perfected — perfect active: the perfecting stands as a completed act with ongoing results): tous hagiazomenous: those being sanctified — present passive participle: the community's sanctification is an ongoing process, but it rests on the completed perfecting act of the one offering. The perfect teteleiōken + present hagiazomenous holds together the finished work and the ongoing application.
15: martyrei de hēmin kai to pneuma to hagion — "the Holy Spirit also bears witness to us concerning this" (martyrei: bears witness, testifies — present tense: the Spirit continues to testify through Scripture): the testimony is ongoing — not merely a past utterance but a present testimony that reaches the community through its reading of the OT. The Spirit who spoke through Jeremiah continues to speak the same word. Hēmin (to us): the first-person plural signals that the scriptural testimony is not merely historical but addressed to the present community.
16: hautē hē diathēkē hēn diathēsomai pros autous meta tas hēmeras ekeinaslegeicurios: didonai nomous mou epi kardias autōn, kai epi tēs dianoias autōn epigrapsō autous — "This is the covenant I will make with them after those days, says the Lord: I will put my laws on their hearts and write them on their minds" (Jeremiah 31:33, repeated from Hebrews 8:10): the repetition of the Jer 31 citation is not redundant but deliberate — it frames the argument about Christ's one sacrifice with the promise of the new covenant's complete internalized forgiveness. Epi kardias... epi tēs dianoias (on hearts... on minds): heart and mind together represent the whole inner person; the law is inscribed on both the affective and cognitive dimensions.
17: kai tōn hamartiōn autōn kai tōn anomiōn autōn ou mē mnēsthō eti — "I will remember their sins and their lawless deeds no more" (Jeremiah 31:34 LXX, final clause): ou mē mnēsthō eti: the emphatic double-negative + aorist subjunctive + eti (still/any longer) — the absolute divine non-recollection. The citation concludes the long scriptural argument: the new covenant achieves what the Levitical system could not — the permanent resolution of the sin-problem through God's deliberate covenant-forgetting. The citation is placed after the description of Christ's completed sacrifice to show: the sacrifice achieves what the Jeremiah promise required.
18: hopou de aphesis toutōn, ouketi prosphora peri hamartias — "where there is forgiveness of these sins, there is no longer any offering for sin" (aphesis: forgiveness, release — the letting-go of the debt/obligation): the logical conclusion drawn from the Jer 31 citation: if sins are no longer remembered by God, no mechanism remains for addressing them. Ouketi prosphora peri hamartias: no longer an offering for sin — the sacrificial system becomes structurally unnecessary once its goal has been achieved. This verse is the pivot from the doctrinal argument (Hebrews 7-Hebrews 10:18) to the parenetic application (Hebrews 10:19-39).
20: hēn enekainisen hēmin hodon prosphaton kai zōsan dia tou katapetasmatos, tout' estin tēs sarkos autou — "by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh" (prosphaton: freshly slaughtered, new — from pros + sphattō: the freshly-killed; prosphatos could mean both newly-made and freshly-sacrificed, which is significant for the sacrificial context): zōsan (living): the way is living because the one who opened it is alive; the death through which access was gained was not the end of the story. Tou katapetasmatos, tout' estin tēs sarkos autou (the curtain, that is, his flesh): the identification of the curtain with Christ's flesh is the interpretive move — the curtain in the tabernacle separated the holy from the most holy; Christ's body was the barrier between humanity and God's presence that his death opened.
21: kai hierea megan epi ton oikon tou theou — "and since we have a great high priest over the house of God" (hierea megan: a great priest — the superlative epithet echoing Hebrews 4:14; epi ton oikon tou theou: over the house of God): the "house of God" picks up the oikos-language of Hebrews 3 (Moses faithful in the house; Christ faithful over the house). The community is both the house and the assembled worshippers who now have access to what the house symbolized.
22: proserchōmetha meta alēthinēs kardias en plērophoria pisteōs, errantismenoi tas kardias apo syneidēseōs ponēras kai leloumenoi to sōma hydati katharō — "let us draw near with a sincere heart and in full assurance of faith, with our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water" (proserchōmetha: let us draw near — hortatory subjunctive echoing Hebrews 4:16; the same verb for priestly approach to the sanctuary): errantismenoi tas kardias (hearts having been sprinkled — perfect passive participle: the sprinkling has already occurred and its state remains): the language maps Old Covenant purification rites onto the inner person: the heart is the locus of the sprinkling. Leloumenoi to sōma hydati katharō (bodies washed with pure water — also perfect passive): the two perfects (sprinkled hearts, washed bodies) describe the completed state that enables the drawing-near.
24: kai katanōomen allēlous eis paroxysmon agapēs kai kalōn ergōn — "and let us consider how to spur one another on to love and good works" (katanōomen: let us observe carefully, consider attentively — same verb as Hebrews 3:1 [consider Jesus]; the verb requires fixing attention on something with intention): paroxysmon: provocation, stimulation, incitement — the noun is from paroxynō: to sharpen to a point, to provoke; the cognate gave us "paroxysm" in English. The provocative stimulation is positive here — goading one another toward love, not toward dispute (Acts 15:39 uses the same word for Paul and Barnabas's sharp disagreement). Agapēs kai kalōn ergōn (love and good works): the two outcomes that the mutual provocation is to produce.
27: phobera de tis ekdochē kriseōs kai pyrōs zēlos esthiein mellontos tous hypenantious — "but a terrifying expectation of judgment and a raging fire that will consume the adversaries of God" (ekdochē: expectation, receiving — waiting to receive something; the word implies the thing awaited is already determined and approaching): pyrōs zēlos: a jealousy/fury of fire — zēlos can mean either jealousy (jealous fire) or zealousness (fierce fire); the genitive describes the fire's quality as consuming-zeal. Tous hypenantious: the adversaries, those who stand against — those who reject the Son become adversaries of God.
28: athetēsas tis nomon Mōyseōs chōris oiktirmōn epi dysin ē trisin martyrsin apothnēskei — "anyone who rejected the law of Moses died without mercy on the evidence of two or three witnesses" (athetēsas: aorist participle of atheteō: to set aside as if nonexistent, to nullify — the same word used in 7:18 for the setting-aside of the commandment): the conditional-analogical structure: if the lesser (Mosaic law-rejection) carried capital punishment without mercy, how much more the greater? Chōris oiktirmōn: without compassions — oiktirmos: the gut-feeling of compassion, the visceral mercy; the court could not allow emotion to override the Deuteronomic death-penalty requirement (Deuteronomy 17:6).
29: posō dokite cheironos axióthēsetai timōrias ho ton huion tou theou katapatēsas, kai to haima tēs diathēkēs koinon hēgēsamenos, en hō hēgiasthē, kai to pneuma tēs charitos enybrisas — "how much worse punishment, do you think, will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God, has treated as something common the blood of the covenant by which he was sanctified, and has insulted the Spirit of grace" (katapatēsas: having trampled underfoot — the image of complete contempt, treating what is most holy as street-dirt): koinon hēgēsamenos (having considered common/profane — koinos: what belongs to everyone, thus defiled, unclean): the covenant blood is considered no better than ordinary blood. Enybrisas: having insulted, outraged — hybrizō: to treat with contempt that demeans; the three aorist participles escalate in severity: trampling → profaning → insulting.
30: oidamen gar ton eiponta, Emoi ekdikēsis, egō antapodōsō; kai palin, Krinei kyrios ton laon autou — "for we know him who said, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, and again, The Lord will judge his people" (Deuteronomy 32:35-36 cited): Emoi ekdikēsis (Vengeance belongs to me — the divine possessive): the citation from the Song of Moses establishes the divine monopoly on recompense. Krinei Kyrios ton laon autou: the Lord judges his people — the judgment is not of outsiders but of the covenant people. The second citation (Deuteronomy 32:36) is a warning that the covenant relationship does not insulate from divine judgment but rather intensifies accountability.
32: anamimNēskesthe de tas proteron hēmeras, en hais phōtisthentes pollēn athlēsin hypemeinete pathēmatōn — "but recall the earlier days when, after you were enlightened, you endured a hard and painful struggle" (anami mnēskesthe: recall, bring back to memory — present imperative: ongoing recollection as a pastoral resource): phōtisthentes (having been enlightened — aorist passive: the completed event of baptism/conversion; the same word as Hebrews 6:4): the community's conversion is the reference point. Athlēsin: an athletic contest, a struggle — athleō: to compete athletically; the metaphor frames the community's persecution-experience as a contest requiring both skill and endurance.
33: touto men oneidismois te kai thlipsesin theatrizomenoi, touto de koinōnoi tōn houtōs anastrophentōn genēthentes — "sometimes being publicly exposed to reproach and affliction, and sometimes standing alongside those who were treated this way" (theatrizomenoi: being made a spectacle in the theater — theatron: theater, public display; the passive: being put on display for the public's entertainment): the Roman practice of public shaming and spectacle-punishment is the background. Koinōnoi: sharers, partners — the community's solidarity with those suffering was itself an act of faith that incurred risk; identifying with the condemned made one a target.
34: kai gar tois desmiois synepathēsate kai tēn harpagēn tōn hyparchontōn hymōn meta charas prosedexasthe — "for you showed compassion to those in prison and joyfully accepted the plundering of your property" (tois desmiois synepathēsate: you suffered-with the prisoners — synpatheō: shared-suffering, co-suffering; the same root as Hebrews 4:15 [the Son sympathizes with weaknesses]): harpagēn: plundering, seizure — the goods were forcibly taken, not voluntarily surrendered; the community's joy was not in the loss itself but in the enduring possession. Ginōskontes echein heautous kreittōna hyparxin kai menousan (knowing that you yourselves have a better, abiding possession): the consciousness of the better possession transforms the attitude toward the lost one.
35: mē apobalēte oun tēn parrēsian hymōn, hētis echei megalēn misthapodosian — "therefore do not throw away your confidence, which carries a great reward" (apobalēte: aorist subjunctive of apoballō: to throw away, to cast off — a single decisive act of discarding): the parrēsia that was given at conversion and maintained through persecution must not be voluntarily discarded at the end. Misthapodosian: recompense, reward — the same word as Hebrews 2:2 (the law's just misthapodosia); now the positive reward of faithful perseverance rather than the penalty of transgression. The parrēsia is worth keeping because what it carries is of greater value than what one might gain by abandoning it.
36: hypomonēs gar echete chreian, hina to thelēma tou theou poiēsantes komizēsthe tēn epangelian — "for you need endurance, so that when you have done the will of God you may receive what was promised" (hypomonēs: endurance, the staying-power under pressure — hypo: under + menō: to remain; the virtue of remaining in place under the weight): komizēsthe: may receive, carry off — komizō: originally to care for and carry; the middle voice suggests personal reception of something belonging to oneself. The endurance must first produce the will-doing, which then receives the promise.
37: eti gar mikron hoson hoson, ho erchomenos hēxei kai ou chronizei — "for yet a little while, and the coming one will come and will not delay" (Isaiah 26:20 + Habakkuk 2:3 combined in LXX): mikron hoson hoson: a very little while — the double hoson intensifies the brevity; a Hebraism for "just a tiny moment"; ho erchomenos: the coming one — the Habakkuk text in the LXX has a person ("he will come") rather than the MT's "it" (the vision); Hebrews follows the LXX and applies the "coming one" to Christ. Ou chronizei: will not delay — chronizō: to linger in time, to be late; the coming is certain and near.
39: hēmeis de ouk esmen hypostolēs eis apōleian, alla pisteōs eis peripoiēsin psychēs — "but we do not belong to those who shrink back and are destroyed, but to those who have faith and so preserve their souls" (hypostolēs: shrinking back — genitive; from hypostellō: to take in the sails, to shorten, to draw back; the nautical image of a ship shortening its sails in fear): eis apōleian: unto destruction/loss — the final state of the shrinking-back; pisteōs eis peripoiēsin psychēs (faith unto the preservation/acquisition of the soul — peripoiēsis: the acquiring for oneself, the preservation; from peri + poieō: to make-around, to secure). The community's self-definition is constituted by belonging to the faith-group, not the shrinking-back group.
